summary: I was born in Japan in Meiji 18, which is 1885. My family served under the Saga feudal lord, and I grew up in a strict household with strong moral teachings. I received some education there, learning from my father who was a calligrapher and poet. Later, I left Japan to join my husband in America in 1915.

My father and maternal grandfather both served under the Saga Domain, the feudal lordship in our area. This status placed our family in a higher social class, and we were expected to uphold strict discipline and respect. Growing up in this environment shaped much of my early life and values.

I probably learned about Sontoku Ninomiya when I was young, but my memory is not very clear now. He was a scholar known in our village, and I think his teachings were part of the moral education we received. However, I cannot recall specific lessons about him.

My parents were followers of the Zenshū Buddhist sect. My father had a morning ritual where he prayed facing all directions and then worshipped the God of Water before entering the house. This religious practice was a significant part of our family life in Japan.

I became a member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church when I was 47 years old after my husband passed away. A minister named Rev. Nagaki came to my home to teach me about nutrition and the Bible, which helped me regain my health. Since then, I have been a faithful member for almost 45 years and found strength in my faith during difficult times.

After living in Seattle, we moved to Mount View, California, where my husband passed away in 1950. We lived there from 1924 until 1938 before relocating to Oakland. My husband’s death was a difficult time, but I stayed strong to raise our children in Mount View.

My elder sister lived in Nagasaki city, and she helped arrange my marriage by introducing me to my husband. Although I was from Saga and he was from Shikoku, Nagasaki was the city where our families connected. It played an important role in bringing us together.

My husband was originally from Iyo in Shikoku Island. He came to America to study and later worked for the U.S. Government. His background from Shikoku was part of his identity, though we lived mostly in Seattle and California after marriage.

I came to Seattle in 1915 to join my husband who was working there for the U.S. Government. We lived in Seattle for about nine years, and all my children except the eldest were born there. I thought Seattle was a nice place and had many Japanese stores which made life easier for me despite not speaking English well.

In 1938, we moved from Mount View to Oakland, California. This was a new chapter for our family after many years in Seattle and Mount View. I lived in Oakland with my children until the war years and beyond.

When Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941, I was living in California. My memory is not very clear about the exact details, but I know my son and I were evacuated soon after. This event changed our lives drastically as we were sent to assembly and relocation centers during the war.

My son and I were sent to the Walerga Assembly Center after the evacuation order during World War II. It was the first place we stayed before being moved to other relocation centers. This was a difficult time but we tried to keep our spirits up.

After Walerga, we were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center. My son served in the military from there before Tule Lake became a segregated center. I stayed out of the political troubles there and focused on supporting others and my family.

Later, I was moved to the Topaz War Relocation Center after Tule Lake became segregated. I stayed there for a while and continued my faith and community work despite the hardships. My daughter was sent to Minidoka, so we were separated during this time.

My daughter was sent to the Minidoka War Relocation Center during the war while I was at Topaz. We were separated but kept in touch as best we could. It was a hard time for all of us, but we tried to stay strong through our faith and family bonds.

I remember the Japanese–Chinese War from when I was young, though I was still a child at the time. It was part of the history I learned growing up in Japan, but I did not experience it directly. It was a distant event that shaped the times.
I also remember hearing about the Japanese–Russian War when I was young, but I was very young and do not recall many details. It was part of the background history during my early years in Japan.

My husband worked for the United States Government after coming to America to study. He was employed in Seattle and later in California. He was not a representative of the Japanese Government, and his job helped support our family in the U.S.
